text: The initial thought was that the lesion was involved with the middle lobe and the plan was to perform a right middle lobectomy.
However, interoperatively, you can see the mass here fairly mobile and not adhere to the lung at all.
It is adjacent to the right heart border.
There are some adhesions of the middle lobe to the mass, which were easily taken down using bipolar dissection.
There was this one area here where there was a little bit more middle lobe stuck to the mass, and I thought that the use of a stapler would be best to free this up.
Once this was done, there was some pericardial fat which was adherent to the mass.
There appeared to be some blood vessels likely supplying blood flow to the mass, which were taken down using the bipolar dissection.
Here again, we're coming around the pericardial fat, just trying to free up the mass from the pericardial fat.
And once this is done, the mass is completely separated and freed.
A bag is then used to retrieve the specimen, which is then removed and sent to pathology.
This patient did very well postoperatively and was discharged home on postoperative number one.
